stem: A row of [[n]] flags is to be displayed, made up of [[a]] identical red flags, [[b]] identical blue flags, and [[c]] identical green flags. How many distinguishable arrangements of the [[n]] flags in a row are possible?
solution: With repeated identical items the count is \( \frac{[[n]]!}{[[a]]!\,[[b]]!\,[[c]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats all [[n]] flags as distinct and forgets to divide by the repeats.
This divides by the red and blue repeats but forgets the green repeats.
This adds the factorials in the denominator instead of multiplying them.
This counts only the ways to place the red flags, ignoring blue vs. green.
This is off from the correct count by the number of red flags.
This is off from the correct count by the number of blue flags.

stem: A row of [[n]] tiles is formed using [[nA]] identical red tiles and [[nB]] identical blue tiles, arranged so that NO TWO red tiles are adjacent. How many distinguishable arrangements are possible?
solution: Place the [[nB]] identical blue tiles first; this creates \( [[nB]]+1 \) gaps (including the two ends) in which to place red tiles, at most one per gap so no two reds touch. Choosing [[nA]] of these gaps gives \( \binom{[[nB]]+1}{[[nA]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the non-adjacency requirement and counts all multiset arrangements.
This uses only [[nB]] gaps instead of the correct \( [[nB]]+1 \) (forgetting the two end gaps).
This uses one fewer gap than even the previous mistake.
This multiplies the two tile counts, an unrelated formula.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: A row of [[n]] flags is formed from [[a]] identical red flags, [[b]] identical blue flags, and [[c]] identical green flags. If the FIRST flag in the row must be red and the LAST flag must be green, how many distinguishable arrangements of the entire row are possible?
solution: Fix one red flag first and one green flag last; the remaining \( [[n]]-2 \) positions hold the leftover \( [[a]]-1 \) red, [[b]] blue, and \( [[c]]-1 \) green flags: \( \dfrac{([[n]]-2)!}{([[a]]-1)!\,[[b]]!\,([[c]]-1)!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the fixed endpoints entirely and counts all arrangements of the full row.
This shortens the row for the fixed ends but forgets to also remove one red and one green flag from the counts.
This only shortens the row by one position instead of two.
This mistakenly also reduces the blue flag count, which was not fixed at either end.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.

stem: A row of [[n]] tiles is formed using [[nA]] identical red tiles and [[nB]] identical blue tiles, with the requirement that ALL [[nA]] red tiles appear together as a single contiguous block. How many distinguishable arrangements of the row are possible?
solution: Treat the block of [[nA]] red tiles as a single unit; together with the [[nB]] identical blue tiles there are \( [[nB]]+1 \) units in a row, so the block can go in any of \( [[nB]]+1 \) positions among them: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This ignores the together-requirement and counts all multiset arrangements.
This reports the total tile count instead of the number of block positions.
This mistakenly treats the [[nB]] blue tiles as distinguishable from each other.
This mistakenly multiplies by the red-tile count as if the block itself had internal orderings.
This is off from the correct count by one.
This is off from the correct count by one.
